entities.name: url-base-vs-path-separation
observations: URLs split into two parts that must NEVER be mixed: BASE (scheme + host + port + base prefix; environment-specific; lives in .env files; varies between dev/staging/prod) and PATH (endpoint route + query string; code-specific; defined by the HTTP client, route file, or OpenAPI spec; same across environments). When agents or developers confuse the two, they patch code to fix what is actually an env config issue, or patch .env to fix what is actually a code/contract issue.

Diagnostic heuristic on a 4xx, 5xx, or connection failure: classify the symptom before changing anything. (a) Wrong host / port / scheme -> BASE problem -> fix the .env of that environment, do NOT touch code. (b) Wrong path / method / query / payload -> PATH problem -> fix the HTTP client, route handler, or OpenAPI contract, do NOT touch .env. (c) Path is correct in code AND .env is correct, but the gateway (Apigee, ingress, BFF) returns 400 / OASValidation / route-not-found while the backend accepts the same call -> spec / contract not re-registered at the gateway; bump OpenAPI info.version and re-import, NOT patch the URL in client code.

Co-symptom: when a backend service sits behind an API gateway like Apigee that imports the OpenAPI spec by version string, every change that adds, removes, or modifies a path MUST bump info.version in the same commit. If info.version stays the same, the gateway skips re-import and the new endpoint stays unregistered, producing 400s at the gateway even though the backend itself works. This pairs with the BASE-vs-PATH rule because the typical wrong fix is to patch the URL in client code (treating the symptom as a path problem) when it is actually a contract-registration problem.

Authoring rules. (1) Code MUST NOT hardcode BASE -- read it from an env var (API_BASE_URL, <SERVICE>_URL, etc.). (2) .env* files MUST NOT contain endpoint paths -- only BASE. (3) A PR that modifies endpoint paths MUST NOT also modify .env*; a PR that changes environment config MUST NOT also modify route handlers or HTTP clients. If both appear in the same diff, it usually means the author confused BASE with PATH; require justification before merging.

Reviewer severity guidance. Hardcoded scheme / host / port in code -> CRITICAL (blocks per-environment deploy). Endpoint paths inside .env* -> CRITICAL (breaks the assumption that envs are interchangeable). Mixed path + .env changes in one PR without justification -> SUGGESTION (request a split or explicit reason). Adding endpoints behind a gateway without bumping the OpenAPI info.version -> CRITICAL (the deploy will 400 at the gateway even after merge).

Common confusion patterns observed in agent flows. (a) Agent sees a 400 at the gateway, assumes the URL in the HTTP client is wrong, edits the client to point at a different path -- masks the real problem (missing version bump) and breaks the contract once it is re-imported. (b) Agent debugging a connection refusal copies a host name into the route file as a hardcoded string instead of fixing API_BASE_URL in .env -- the fix appears to work in dev but breaks in staging/prod. (c) Agent treating .env edits as code changes -- it commits an env-only tweak as if it were a feature, and reviewers waste cycles parsing the diff. Mitigation: enforce the BASE-vs-PATH split and the one-concern-per-PR rule; reviewers must reject diffs that mix both without an explicit reason.
